skill.name: yolo
skill.description: Automates Lovable deployment workflows with browser automation, post-push detection, deployment prompts, and verification checks for edge functions and migrations.
skill.summary: Automates Lovable deployments and verification from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code workflows.
skill.author: 10K-Digital

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution alerts are mostly markdown and pseudocode false positives, but the skill semantically directs an agent to perform authenticated browser automation and automatic Lovable deployments after git push. No confirmed malicious exfiltration or prompt injection was found, but the combination of deployment side effects, local configuration reads, secret-name inspection, and outbound tests makes this high risk for marketplace publication without strong warnings and permission gates.

high_findings.title: Automatic Deployment After Git Push
high_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to detect a successful git push to main and proceed with Lovable deployment when auto_deploy is enabled. This can trigger production or cloud-side changes without a fresh per-deployment confirmation, so a misconfiguration or accidental push can cause unintended deployments.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicitly documented as automatic after git push and includes deployment execution steps. It is a legitimate feature, but the side effect is high impact.

high_findings.title: Authenticated Browser Automation Against Lovable
high_findings.description: The skill directs the agent to use an authenticated browser session to navigate Lovable, submit deployment prompts, wait for responses, and continue after login. Actions occur in the user account context and can affect cloud deployment state.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow repeatedly requires logged-in Lovable access and automated prompt submission. The risk is clear, although the intent is deployment automation rather than abuse.

medium_findings.title: Secret Name and Configuration Discovery
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to inspect Lovable Cloud settings and extract configured secret names, including names such as API keys and service-role keys. It says to ignore masked values, so no secret-value exfiltration was found, but the inventory itself is sensitive configuration metadata.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced workflow explicitly extracts and returns secret names while stating that masked values are ignored. This supports a medium confidentiality risk, not confirmed credential theft.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Functional Tests Can Trigger Live Side Effects
medium_findings.description: The testing workflow builds Supabase edge-function URLs and sends HTTP requests with an authorization header and test payload. It includes a payment-function skip rule, but other functions such as email sending can still perform live side effects during verification.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow clearly instructs HTTP POST verification against deployed endpoints. It includes some safeguards, so the risk is elevated but bounded.

medium_findings.title: Local Repository and Project Metadata Inspection
medium_findings.description: The skill reads CLAUDE.md, git change ranges, Supabase client files, migration paths, and backend file names to decide what to deploy. This is needed for the workflow, but it increases exposure of project structure and deployment context to automated prompts and logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file and git inspection steps are explicit and central to the workflow. No direct leak was found, but the data is sensitive operational context.

low_findings.title: Static Command Alerts Are Mostly Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: Most static external-command alerts point to inline command names, code fences, or pseudocode in markdown reference files. They are not executable Ruby backticks or runnable scripts in this skill package, so they are largely false positives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation and command examples rather than executable code. The semantic workflow still has operational risk, which is covered in higher-severity findings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Code Execution Plus Network Plus Credential Metadata Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines git command workflows, browser automation to Lovable, cloud secret-name inspection, and outbound function tests. This matches the static analyzer heuristic, but the evidence indicates high-risk automation rather than confirmed malicious intent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All three behavior classes are present in the documentation. The combination is risky, but the text frames it as deployment support and avoids extracting secret values.

dangerous_patterns.title: Autonomous Cloud-Side State Changes
dangerous_patterns.description: Auto-deploy mode proceeds from a successful git push to deployment prompts for edge functions and migrations. This can change production behavior or database state through an authenticated third-party UI.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The deployment trigger and browser automation path are explicit. The risk depends on user configuration but is a direct intended capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Configuration Enumeration
dangerous_patterns.description: The secrets workflow enumerates configured secret names from Lovable Cloud settings. Even without values, secret names can reveal integrations, vendors, and privileged service-role usage.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly returns names such as API keys and service-role keys. It also explicitly avoids masked values, limiting severity.

content.value_statement: Manual Lovable deployments slow down teams that ship Supabase edge functions and migrations often. This skill automates deployment prompts, sync checks, and verification steps from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Detects backend changes in Supabase functions and migration folders after git push.
Opens Lovable project pages through browser automation and submits deployment prompts.
Waits for GitHub sync before starting deployment prompts.
Runs configurable deployment checks, console checks, and functional endpoint tests.
Extracts Lovable Cloud secret names to compare configured and missing secrets.
Provides manual fallback prompts when browser automation fails.
limitations: Requires an authenticated Lovable browser session and compatible browser automation tools.
Can break if the Lovable user interface or selectors change.
Focused on Supabase edge functions and migrations, not all Lovable project operations.
Auto-deploy mode can perform high-impact actions and needs strict user review.

use_cases.title: Speed Up Edge Function Deployment
use_cases.description: Deploy changed Supabase edge functions through Lovable after pushing code to main.

use_cases.title: Verify Migration Application
use_cases.description: Apply pending Supabase migrations and confirm schema changes with post-deployment checks.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Deployment Flow
use_cases.description: Give a team a repeatable Lovable deployment process with progress updates and fallback instructions.

prompt_templates.title: Enable Guided Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use yolo mode to deploy my latest Supabase edge function through Lovable. Keep testing enabled and ask before any risky action.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy One Edge Function
prompt_templates.prompt: Deploy the changed edge function in supabase/functions through Lovable, wait for GitHub sync, and report the deployment result.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Pending Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Check my new Supabase migration, warn me about destructive operations, then apply it through Lovable after I confirm.

prompt_templates.title: Run Full Post-Push Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: After my successful push to main, detect changed backend files, queue needed Lovable deployments, verify sync, run tests, and summarize failures.

output_examples.input: Deploy the send-email edge function with testing on.
output: Reports each automation step from Lovable navigation through prompt submission.
Confirms whether Lovable accepted the deployment request.
Runs verification checks and lists any missing secrets or endpoint failures.

output_examples.input: Auto-deploy backend changes after pushing to main.
output: Identifies modified edge functions and migration files.
Waits for Lovable to show the pushed commit before submitting prompts.
Returns a deployment summary with manual fallback steps if automation fails.
Opens Lovable Cloud settings through the browser session.
Lists detected secret names without collecting secret values.
Highlights missing secrets needed by deployed functions.

best_practices: Keep auto-deploy disabled for production unless every deployment is reviewed by a human.
Use safe test payloads and skip tests that can charge cards, send real messages, or mutate important data.
Review secret-name output and logs before sharing deployment summaries outside the project team.
anti_patterns: Do not enable auto-deploy on repositories where main is not production-ready.
Do not use this skill with accounts that have broader Lovable access than the project needs.
Do not treat successful prompt submission as proof that the deployed feature is safe.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy code by itself?
faq.answer: It can when yolo mode and auto-deploy are enabled. That behavior should require clear user approval and careful configuration.

faq.question: Does it read secret values?
faq.answer: No evidence found that it intentionally reads secret values. It does inspect and list secret names from Lovable Cloud.

faq.question: Which platforms does it target?
faq.answer: It targets Lovable projects that use Supabase edge functions and migrations.

faq.question: Can it work without browser automation?
faq.answer: It falls back to manual Lovable prompts when browser automation is unavailable or the UI cannot be found.

faq.question: Why is the security rating high?
faq.answer: The skill combines authenticated browser actions, automatic deployments, local project inspection, and outbound endpoint tests.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production deployments?
faq.answer: Only with strict review gates. Auto-deploy mode is risky for production because it can change live services after a push.
